verses.text: Jehoshaphat, rich and honoured, makes a marriage alliance with wicked Ahab and agrees to join him at Ramoth-gilead. The godly king binds himself to an ungodly one. Unequal alliances, however flattering, draw even good men into other men's sins.

Jehoshaphat, rich and honoured, makes a marriage alliance with wicked Ahab and agrees to join him at Ramoth-gilead. The godly king binds himself to an ungodly one. Unequal alliances, however flattering, draw even good men into other men's sins.

Jehoshaphat, rich and honoured, makes a marriage alliance with wicked Ahab and agrees to join him at Ramoth-gilead. The godly king binds himself to an ungodly one. Unequal alliances, however flattering, draw even good men into other men's sins.

Jehoshaphat asks to inquire of the LORD first; Ahab's four hundred prophets promise success, but the king of Judah wants a true prophet, and Ahab admits he hates Micaiah for never prophesying good of him. A crowd of yes-men cannot replace one faithful voice. Truth is hated only by those bent on wrong.

A crowd of yes-men cannot replace one faithful voice. Truth is hated only by those bent on wrong.

A crowd of yes-men cannot replace one faithful voice. Truth is hated only by those bent on wrong.

Jehoshaphat asks to inquire of the LORD first; Ahab's four hundred prophets promise success, but the king of Judah wants a true prophet, and Ahab admits he hates Micaiah for never prophesying good of him. A crowd of yes-men cannot replace one faithful voice. Truth is hated only by those bent on wrong.

Micaiah is summoned while the prophets, led by Zedekiah with his iron horns, all promise victory. Flattery wears the dress of prophecy. Men can multiply assurances of success when their words only echo what the king already wants to hear.

Micaiah is summoned while the prophets, led by Zedekiah with his iron horns, all promise victory. Flattery wears the dress of prophecy. Men can multiply assurances of success when their words only echo what the king already wants to hear.

Micaiah is summoned while the prophets, led by Zedekiah with his iron horns, all promise victory. Flattery wears the dress of prophecy. Men can multiply assurances of success when their words only echo what the king already wants to hear.

The messenger urges Micaiah to agree with the rest, but he answers, 'what my God saith, that will I speak.' He will not bend the message to please. The faithful servant fears God more than the frown of kings, and speaks only what is true.

The messenger urges Micaiah to agree with the rest, but he answers, 'what my God saith, that will I speak.' He will not bend the message to please. The faithful servant fears God more than the frown of kings, and speaks only what is true.

Micaiah first mocks their false assurance, then foretells Israel scattered 'as sheep that have no shepherd' — and Ahab grumbles he never prophesies good. The plain truth at last breaks through. God's word will not always soothe; sometimes it must warn of the ruin pride is rushing toward.

Micaiah reveals his vision: the LORD permitting a lying spirit in the mouths of Ahab's prophets to lure him to his fall. God in justice gives the self-deceived the deception they prefer. Those who will not love the truth are at last left to believe a lie.

Zedekiah strikes him and Ahab jails Micaiah on bread and water; he answers, 'if thou return at all in peace, the LORD hath not spoken by me.' The true prophet stakes all on God's word. Persecution cannot unsay the truth, and time will prove whose message was from heaven.

Ahab goes disguised while Jehoshaphat wears his robes; the Syrians chase Jehoshaphat until he cries out, and God moves them away from him. The disguise cannot outwit God, but mercy spares the foolish ally who calls on the LORD. He hears the cry even of those caught by their own mistakes.

Ahab goes disguised while Jehoshaphat wears his robes; the Syrians chase Jehoshaphat until he cries out, and God moves them away from him. The disguise cannot outwit God, but mercy spares the foolish ally who calls on the LORD. He hears the cry even of those caught by their own mistakes.

A man draws a bow 'at a venture' and the random arrow finds Ahab between the joints of his armour; he props himself up and dies at evening. No disguise or armour can dodge God's appointed stroke. What men call chance is the sure aim of His justice.
